content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>RefreshRouting command implementation and routing table generation logic</title>
  <subtitle>Examined /refreshrouting command structure revealing intelligent merge strategy for NotebookLM routing table updates</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>REFRESH_ROUTING_PROMPT defines complete routing table refresh workflow using ms-notebooklm-routing-builder skill</fact>
    <fact>Command calls notebook_list to fetch current notebooks and reads existing notebooklm-routing.json from MEMORY_DIR/config for differential update</fact>
    <fact>Backup strategy preserves last 5 versions at MEMORY_DIR/config/notebooklm-routing.bak.[timestamp].json before writing new file</fact>
    <fact>Each notebook generates 100-300 character repr text via notebook_query containing core terms, names, and domain vocabulary for embedding vectorization</fact>
    <fact>Merge logic preserves manual configuration: new notebooks append with strategy=primary, existing entries only update repr/last_refreshed, strategy/strategy_notes/manual_overrides never overwritten</fact>
    <fact>Deleted notebooks marked with removed=true flag rather than hard deletion to preserve history</fact>
    <fact>multi_match_rules section preserved unchanged during refresh to protect custom routing rules</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The /refreshrouting command is implemented in memory.ts as handleRefreshRouting function. When executed via Telegram, it spawns a Kiro agent session with REFRESH_ROUTING_PROMPT that orchestrates a sophisticated incremental update process. The workflow uses the ms-notebooklm-routing-builder skill to: (1) fetch current NotebookLM state via notebook_list, (2) read existing routing file for differential comparison, (3) backup old version with timestamp retention policy, (4) generate repr descriptions for each notebook using notebook_query that capture domain-specific vocabulary suitable for embedding-based routing, and (5) intelligently merge changes while preserving all manual overrides and strategy configurations. The merge strategy treats the routing file as living configuration: new notebooks get default strategy, existing entries only refresh their content representations, removed notebooks are flagged rather than deleted to maintain audit trail, and multi-match rules remain untouched. Output is summarized in Traditional Chinese without dumping raw JSON. This design balances automation with manual control—the system can refresh content representations as notebooks evolve while respecting human-configured routing strategies.</narrative>

This is interesting. The user is trying to check MCP server configurations, but the PowerShell command encountered a JSON parsing error when trying to read ~/.claude.json. The error shows that the JSON is malformed at position 30955.

However, from the stdout, we can see a massive amount of configuration data that was partially parsed before the error. This gives us visibility into the user's Claude Code configuration, including:

1. No .mcp.json in the project directory
2. The ~/.claude.json file has a malformed JSON structure
3. The output shows extensive feature flags, settings, and configuration data before the parse error

The key finding here is that the ConvertFrom-Json cmdlet failed, which means the MCP servers section couldn't be properly read. This is relevant to the investigation because the /refreshrouting command needs NotebookLM MCP tools (notebook_list, notebook_query), and if the MCP configuration is malformed, those tools might not be properly registered.

Let me record this as a discovery observation about the MCP configuration issue.

1. First they tried to grep for "notebook" in ~/.claude.json but got no results (mode: content, numFiles: 0). This likely means the file is actually binary or has encoding issues, or grep couldn't read it properly.

   - Various CLI MCP configuration locations for NotebookLM:
     - ~/.kiro/settings/mcp.json - doesn't exist
     - ~/.kiro/mcp.json - doesn't exist  
     - ~/.codex/config.toml - exists but no "notebook" references
     - ~/.claude/settings.json - exists but no "notebook" references

So the NotebookLM MCP server is not configured in any of the expected locations. This confirms that the NotebookLM tools are not available because the MCP server was never set up.

This is a key finding: the /refreshrouting command expects NotebookLM MCP tools to be available, but they were never configured in the first place. The routing table file was never generated because the infrastructure to generate it (NotebookLM MCP integration) was never installed/configured.

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>NotebookLM MCP server never configured in any Claude agent CLI</title>
  <subtitle>Comprehensive search across all CLI configuration files confirms no NotebookLM MCP server registration exists anywhere in the system</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Bridge uses claude-agent-acp (ACP_AGENT_COMMAND) with claude-fable-5 model and medium effort level per .env configuration</fact>
    <fact>Kiro CLI MCP configuration files do not exist at expected locations ~/.kiro/settings/mcp.json and ~/.kiro/mcp.json</fact>

    <fact>No MCP server registration for NotebookLM found in any CLI tool configuration across the entire system</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Deep investigation into MCP server availability reveals the root cause is not a malformed configuration but complete absence of NotebookLM MCP server setup. The bridge spawns Kiro agent sessions using claude-agent-acp CLI with Fable 5 model, but neither Kiro CLI nor any other agent CLI (Codex, Claude Code) has NotebookLM MCP server configured. The /refreshrouting command's REFRESH_ROUTING_PROMPT explicitly calls notebook_list and notebook_query tools that would be provided by a NotebookLM MCP server, but that server was never installed or registered. This explains the entire chain: no MCP server configured → no NotebookLM tools available → ToolSearch returns empty for notebook_list/notebook_query → /refreshrouting cannot execute → notebooklm-routing.json never generated → embed router never becomes ready. The earlier ~/.claude.json parsing error is unrelated—even if that JSON were valid, it wouldn't help because no mcpServers entry for NotebookLM exists anywhere. The solution requires installing and configuring a NotebookLM MCP server in the appropriate CLI's configuration before /refreshrouting can work.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>NotebookLM MCP setup script targets wrong CLI - architectural mismatch discovered</title>
  <subtitle>Setup script configures Codex CLI but bridge spawns agents using claude-agent-acp CLI creating configuration mismatch preventing tool availability</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>setup-local-notebooklm-mcp.mjs copies notebooklm-mcp-server from global npm to local directory (default F:\AI_Codex\notebooklm-mcp-server or env LOCAL_NOTEBOOKLM_MCP_DIR)</fact>
    <fact>Setup script modifies ~/.codex/config.toml adding [mcp_servers.notebooklm] section with node command pointing to dist/index.js entrypoint</fact>
    <fact>Bridge .env configuration specifies ACP_AGENT_COMMAND=claude-agent-acp meaning agents spawn using claude-agent-acp CLI not Codex CLI</fact>
    <fact>Codex config.toml exists but contains no notebook configuration confirming setup script has not been executed</fact>
    <fact>Script vendors local copy from global notebooklm-mcp-server@3.0.7 with custom patches for Video Overview RPC and studio_poll video media_url extraction</fact>
    <fact>No MCP server directories found in ~/mcp-servers except cocos362-mcp-server</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Critical architectural mismatch uncovered: the setup-local-notebooklm-mcp.mjs script is designed to configure NotebookLM MCP server for Codex CLI by writing to ~/.codex/config.toml, but the bridge's .env explicitly configures ACP_AGENT_COMMAND=claude-agent-acp, meaning spawned Kiro agent sessions use the claude-agent-acp CLI instead. When a user invokes /refreshrouting via Telegram, the bridge spawns a Kiro agent session using claude-agent-acp, which loads MCP configuration from its own config files (likely ~/.kiro/mcp.json or similar), not from Codex's config.toml. Even if the setup script were executed successfully, it would configure the wrong CLI—the NotebookLM tools would be available in Codex sessions but not in the claude-agent-acp sessions that the bridge actually spawns. The solution requires either: (1) modifying setup script to target claude-agent-acp's MCP configuration location, (2) changing bridge .env to use Codex CLI instead of claude-agent-acp, or (3) manually configuring NotebookLM MCP server in the claude-agent-acp configuration. The script does reveal the correct MCP server package (notebooklm-mcp-server) needs to be installed globally first, then vendored locally with command="node" and args pointing to dist/index.js.</narrative>

The entire integration chain is blocked at the most fundamental level—there is no MCP server binary to configure regardless of which CLI is targeted. The setup script explicitly checks for global notebooklm-mcp-server installation and throws "Global notebooklm-mcp-server not found" error if missing, so attempting to run it would fail immediately.
